terms.definition: Without action, inaction. State of avoiding duty through laziness, fear, or false renunciation; hacker metaphor: A process stopped but still consuming resources.
not disturbed, undisturbed; lit. un-struck, un-violated — in Sāṅkhya terms, the puruṣa (conscious witness) remains unaffected by the perpetual injections of prakṛtic activity
terms.roots: from pāpa (strike) + viddha (struck) with privative a-

terms.definition: Detachment: Operating without the hooks of expectation. Recognition that the actor and the fruits are in different categories, even while the action continues.
terms.roots: from saj (to cling, attach) with privative a-

terms.definition: Imperishable, inexhaustible. The soul's fundamental quality: it cannot be diminished by birth, confounded by death, or fragmented across time. Like root access to the system — credentials that persist across reboots.
terms.roots: from a (privative prefix) + vya (to decay, perish)

terms.definition: Turned to ashes; complete erasure of legacy karmic debt. In Sāṅkhya: the dissolution of the false identification of the soul with the material products of action.
O descendant of Bharata — appellation invoking Arjuna's paternal lineage; reminder of the greatness of the Bharata dynasty

Birth. In classical Sāṅkhya: not the origin of the soul (which is eternal), but the assumption of a new instrument — the body-mind complex — through which puruṣa falsely identifies as agent and enjoyer. The phala (fruit) of prakṛti's cycle, binding until knowledge dissolves the bond.
terms.roots: from jan (to be born, to arise)

terms.definition: The fire of knowledge; the ultimate debugging tool that removes the illusion of separation. Not a conceptual understanding, but a transformative realization that consumes the fuel of desire.
O son of Kuntī — appellation invoking Arjuna's maternal lineage; reminder of the duty that comes from noble birth
Action, work, performance of duty. From root kr (to do); hacker metaphor: The executable—action that produces output in the system.
The yoga of action; Patanjali's term (YS II.1) for the path integrating tapas, svādhyāya, and īśvara-praṇidhānāna.
Prescribed, regulated, bound by duty. Dharma-specific action aligned with one's position; hacker metaphor: The code compiled for your specific architecture.
terms.roots: from ni (down, into) + yam (to restrain, regulate)

terms.definition: The Supreme: For the bhakta, the Personality of Godhead; for the jñānī, liberation. The destination reached when attachment to results is traded for connection to source.

Subduer of the enemy. Arjuna's epithet carries weight here. The enemy to be subdued is not the Kurus on the battlefield — it is the ignorance that makes him forget his nature, his past, his purpose. The warrior who can defeat external armies cannot defeat internal darkness without divine knowledge.
terms.roots: from param (greatest, beyond) + tapa (to heat, to burn)

terms.definition: The fierce, intense, transformative. The yoga indicating purifying fire energy favorable for decisive action and burning away impurities.
terms.roots: from rudra (the fierce/howler, name for Shiva)

terms.definition: Proper execution: Not mere doing, but calibrated action aligned with dharma and timing. The integration of movement toward rightful completion.
ocean, gathering-place of waters — from sam (together) + udra (water). The field containing all phenomena without being modified by them
All reactions to material activities; the accumulated logs of a million previous system states. The totality of karmic debris that must be cleared for root-level liberation.
you must tolerate/endure — active forbearance, not passive suffering; the austere patience that maintains practice through all conditions
mastery, control — from vaś (to be subject to). Not the will-to-power of forceful domination, but the effortless sovereignty of the full
terms.roots: from vaś (to wish, to be subject to) + kāra (making)

terms.definition: Obstacle yoga: An inauspicious combination meaning 'falling away.' The prompt to act with extra care, as wrong action now carries amplified consequence. Not an excuse for paralysis, but a call to precision.
peace, tranquility, cessation of agitation — not the absence of stimulus but the presence of completion. The goal of Sāṅkhya and the fruit of yoga
